headline: Nasdaq and S&P 500 slip while Dow hits record high
summary: STORY: Wall Street's main indexes ended mixed on Tuesday, with the Dow gaining just under two-thirds of a percent to notch its second straight record-high close... while the S&P 500 shed more than half a percent and the Nasdaq dropped more than one percent.Shares of Elon Musk's SpaceX ended nearly 5% higher, paring earlier gains. For much of the session, the rocket and AI company's value was above that of Amazon, and it briefly surpassed Microsoft's valuation, as retail investors continued to pour into the stock after its record-breaking IPO.But Amol Dhargalkar, chairman and managing partner at Chatham Financial, warned of several risks that could trigger volatility for SpaceX and other AI-related stocks."I think that's a really difficult position to be in as a retail investor, especially when you consider the fact that there is a lot of risk, not just in the business model, but these are names where the cash flows are likely far into the future. And we are not in a period of 0% or negative interest rates or even declining interest rates at this point. There's a lot of question as to whether interest rates will continue to be flat or will they go up. And that can certainly be a meaningful drag on valuations of high growth companies, especially where that growth is out far into the future."Investors will look for clues on where interest rates are headed when the Federal Reserve concludes its latest two-day policy meeting on Wednesday, with Kevin Warsh holding his first press conference as Fed chair.Other stocks on the move included shares of Olin, which sank nearly 6% after the chemical producer said it would acquire Huntsman in an all-stock deal valued at more than $2.4 billion. Huntsman shares also fell as the offer stood at a discount to the stock's recent price.:: Yum BrandsAnd shares of Yum Brands rose after the fast-food company said it would sell its Pizza Hut chain for $2.7 billion, against a backdrop of softer consumer spending across the fast-food industry.

headline: Tech Weekly: A blockbuster IPO, a sweeping online block
summary: STORY: From a record-breaking IPO to a far-reaching online ban, this is Tech Weekly. :: Tech WeeklySpaceX on Thursday priced the biggest-ever U.S. initial public offering, raising a record $75 billion. It’s made Elon Musk’s rocket and spacecraft manufacturer one of the world’s most valuable companies.Selling 555.56 million shares at $135 apiece, the company was valued at $1.77 trillion. Thursday’s pricing supports Musk's most ambitious project yet - even as some analysts question whether its lofty valuation is justified.The company said Monday its underwriters had exercised the so-called "greenshoe" option to purchase additional shares.Microsoft has been sued by shareholders who say they've been defrauded. They’ve said the tech giant inflated its stock price by failing to disclose slowing growth in its Azure cloud business.And failing to disclose the need to spend billions of dollars on AI infrastructure. The proposed class action, filed Friday, was prompted by Microsoft shares falling 10% on January 29 after an earnings report.About $357 billion of market value was erased, and Microsoft's stock suffered its biggest one-day decline in nearly six years.Microsoft said on Monday it believes the claims are "without merit”. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Monday he would ban social media sites for under-16s..And impose restrictions on gaming and livestreaming platforms for the age group. This amounts to some of the world's most far-reaching online restrictions, with the ban coming into place by next spring.He said this would help protect children online while pushing back against the power of big technology companies."At the moment, strangers can contact any child unchecked. Just think about that. Is there a situation in the offline world where you would just let your child pair up with a stranger, an adult that you don't know anything about?”Ford and other automakers are scrambling to obtain U.S. government authorization to continue selling certain models - despite being on the market for years. It’s due to a U.S. ban on Chinese-developed and -maintained software in connected vehicles. Ford told Reuters it’s asked the U.S. Commerce Department for authorization to continue importing its China-built Lincoln Nautilus SUV.And said despite its tech being built in the U.S., its installation in China now requires government approval. Lawmakers have ​proposed making the rules even tougher.
